literarySources.title: The Divine Comedy
literarySources.author: Dante Alighieri
literarySources.passage: All hope abandon, ye who enter in.
literarySources.passageContext: Inscription above the gates of Hell — Inferno, Canto III. A warning mirrored in the Sinners' entry into the Limbus Company bus.
literarySources.historicalContext: The foundational structure of Dante's journey through the afterlife provides the narrative blueprint for Limbus Company, from the Manager's name to the division of the City's Districts.

literarySources.title: The Wings
literarySources.author: Yi Sang
literarySources.passage: Have you ever seen a stuffed genius? I am happy. At such a time even my talent, like a stuffed genius, is delightful.
literarySources.passageContext: Opening lines — a reflection on intellectual decay and isolation, echoing Yi Sang's search for identity in the City.
literarySources.historicalContext: Yi Sang's surrealist masterpiece exploring the fragmentation of the self under colonial and existential pressure. The narrator lives in a dark room, dependent on his wife Yeonsim who drugs him with sleeping pills; he escapes and wanders the streets yearning for wings to fly. The wife's name becomes the Mirror's name in Limbus Company.

literarySources.title: Faust
literarySources.author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
literarySources.passage: Two souls, alas, are dwelling in my breast, and one is striving to forsake its brother.
literarySources.passageContext: Faust's internal conflict — the root of his bargain with Mephistopheles and his endless pursuit of knowledge.
literarySources.historicalContext: Goethe's tragedy of the soul's restless search for transcendence. In Limbus Company, it informs Faust's 'omniscient' persona and her potential pact for forbidden knowledge.

literarySources.title: Don Quixote
literarySources.author: Miguel de Cervantes
literarySources.passage: To surrender dreams — this may be madness. To seek treasure where there is only trash — this is true insanity.
literarySources.passageContext: The 'Golden Dream' of the Knight Errant, a delusion that Don Quixote weaponizes against a bleak reality.
literarySources.historicalContext: Cervantes' deconstruction of chivalry. Don Quixote's 'justice' in the City is a direct adaptation of this knightly delusion used to survive a cruel world.

literarySources.title: Hell Screen
literarySources.author: Ryunosuke Akutagawa
literarySources.passage: To paint the screen, I must see the actual sight. If I don't see it, I can't paint it.
literarySources.passageContext: The artist Yoshihide's obsession with perfection, leading to the ultimate sacrifice of his daughter for his art.
literarySources.historicalContext: Akutagawa's exploration of the terrifying cost of artistic perfection. It defines Ryoshu's aesthetic of 'scenic' violence and her detachment from human morality.

literarySources.title: The Stranger (L'Étranger)
literarySources.author: Albert Camus
literarySources.passage: I laid my heart open to the benign indifference of the universe.
literarySources.passageContext: Meursault's final acceptance of the absurd, mirroring his emotional detachment throughout the trial.
literarySources.historicalContext: Camus' manifesto of the absurd. Meursault's role as the 'Stranger' reflects his inability to participate in the City's emotional and social performances.

literarySources.title: Dream of the Red Chamber
literarySources.author: Cao Xueqin
literarySources.passage: When fiction is mistaken for truth, truth becomes fiction; where nothing is taken for something, something becomes nothing.
literarySources.passageContext: A couplet from the Land of Illusion, reflecting the cyclical decay and tragedy of the Jia family.
literarySources.historicalContext: Cao Xueqin's masterpiece on the transience of life and family status. In Project Moon, it underpins Hong Lu's themes of inescapable tragedy and the 'illusion' of his noble background.

literarySources.title: Wuthering Heights
literarySources.author: Emily Brontë
literarySources.passage: I have not broken your heart — you have broken it; and in breaking it, you have broken mine.
literarySources.passageContext: Heathcliff to Catherine, a statement of shared destruction and obsessive vengeance.
literarySources.historicalContext: Brontë's gothic tale of cyclical trauma and revenge. Heathcliff's character in the City is a raw manifestation of this cycle, where love is inseparable from mutual destruction.

literarySources.title: Moby-Dick
literarySources.author: Herman Melville
literarySources.passage: Towards thee I roll, thou all-destroying but unconquering whale; to the last I grapple with thee.
literarySources.passageContext: Captain Ahab's final cry of defiance and obsession, the 'blind vengeance' that Ishmael survives.
literarySources.historicalContext: Melville's epic on the nature of evil and obsession. Ahab's pursuit of the White Whale is the primary engine for Ishmael's Canto, representing a recursive cycle of loss and grapple.

literarySources.title: Crime and Punishment
literarySources.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
literarySources.passage: I did not kill a human being, I killed a principle!
literarySources.passageContext: Raskolnikov's justification for his crime, a philosophy of the 'Extraordinary Man' that leads to his ruin.
literarySources.historicalContext: Dostoevsky's psychological study of sin and redemption. Rodion's story in the City focuses on the heavy cost of 'principled' violence and the search for atonement.

literarySources.title: Demian
literarySources.author: Hermann Hesse
literarySources.passage: The bird fights its way out of the egg. The egg is the world. Who would be born must first destroy a world.
literarySources.passageContext: Demian's lesson to Sinclair about the painful necessity of metamorphosis and breaking free from inherited morality.
literarySources.historicalContext: Hesse's exploration of individuation and the internal 'Abraxas' (the union of god and devil). It defines Sinclair's struggle between the world of light and the world of darkness.

literarySources.title: The Odyssey
literarySources.author: Homer
literarySources.passage: My name is Outis — 'No-man' — and No-man am I called.
literarySources.passageContext: Odysseus's deception of the Cyclops, a moment where identity is discarded to ensure survival and victory.
literarySources.historicalContext: Homer's epic of homecoming and cunning. Outis's character revolves around this 'No-man' identity, using military strategy and deception to survive the City's trials.

literarySources.title: The Metamorphosis
literarySources.author: Franz Kafka
literarySources.passage: I cannot make you understand. I cannot make anyone understand what is happening inside me. I cannot even explain it to myself.
literarySources.passageContext: Gregor Samsa's internal monologue as his humanity decays alongside his insectoid body.
literarySources.historicalContext: Kafka's seminal work on alienation and the absurdity of the human condition. In Project Moon, it serves as the core inspiration for Gregor's physical transformation and his family's subsequent rejection.

literarySources.title: Grimm's Fairy Tales
literarySources.author: Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm
literarySources.passage: The bird was so beautiful, and it sang so sweetly, that the boy forgot his grief.
literarySources.passageContext: From 'The Juniper Tree' — a tale of brutal death and magical rebirth.
literarySources.historicalContext: A collection of folk tales that often contain dark, violent moral lessons. Many Abnormalities in Lobotomy Corporation are 'echoes' of these folk archetypes.

literarySources.title: The Tree of Life (Kabbalah)
literarySources.author: Theological Origin
literarySources.passage: Keter, Chokhmah, Binah, Chesed, Gevurah, Tiferet, Netzach, Hod, Yesod, Malkuth.
literarySources.passageContext: The ten Sephirot of the Kabbalistic Tree, representing the emanation of the Divine. Directly mirrored by the 10 Sephirah-led departments of Lobotomy Corporation.
literarySources.historicalContext: The core philosophical framework for Lobotomy Corp's architecture and the Seed of Light project. Each Sephirah AI is named after a Sephirah on the Tree.

literarySources.title: The Book of Revelations
literarySources.author: Theological Origin
literarySources.passage: And I saw, and behold a white horse: and he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him.
literarySources.passageContext: The vision of the apocalypse and the four horsemen. Echoed by the transformation of Plague Doctor into WhiteNight.
literarySources.historicalContext: Reflected in the figure of the Antichrist and the 12 Apostles in Lobotomy Corp. WhiteNight acts as a messianic figure with absolute authority over its apostles.

literarySources.title: Cain & Abel
literarySources.author: Theological Origin
literarySources.passage: Am I my brother's keeper?
literarySources.passageContext: The first murder and the mark of Cain, representing the dual nature of humanity.
literarySources.historicalContext: Intertwined with Hermann Hesse's Demian. Sinclair's mark and his internal conflict between 'light' and 'dark' worlds are rooted in this biblical archetype.

literarySources.title: Book of Rites (Lǐjì)
literarySources.author: Confucius (Attributed)
literarySources.passage: Manners (Li) are the foundation of all order. Without them, even the most powerful king is but a beast in robes.
literarySources.passageContext: A collection of texts describing the social forms, administration, and ceremonial rites of the Zhou dynasty.
literarySources.historicalContext: One of the Five Classics of the Confucian canon. It serves as the primary ideological inspiration for 'The Thumb' (Ngón Cái) of the Five Fingers, who enforce 'Propriety' with absolute and lethal prejudice.

literarySources.title: Water Margin (Shuǐhǔ Zhuàn)
literarySources.author: Shi Nai'an
literarySources.passage: The 108 Stars of Destiny shall gather at the marsh, bound by brotherhood and the stars above.
literarySources.passageContext: One of the Four Great Classical Novels of Chinese literature, telling the story of 108 outlaws who form a sizable army.
literarySources.historicalContext: The primary literary inspiration for 'The Pinky' (Ngón Út) of the Five Fingers. The Pinky's structure and its use of 'Stars' (like the Tiantui Star) directly mirror the 108 Stars of Destiny from the novel.

literarySources.title: Crow's Eye View (오감도)
literarySources.author: Yi Sang
literarySources.passage: 이상한 가역반응 — Strange reversible reaction: thirteen moments on the angle of a crow's gaze.
literarySources.passageContext: From the 15-poem cycle that scandalized the Korean literary establishment, called 'the sleep talk of a lunatic.' Its anti-realist structure mirrors the Mirror technology's shattering of identity.
literarySources.historicalContext: Yi Sang's most controversial work — a 15-poem cycle incorporating mathematical and scientific language into surrealist verse. The title 'Crow's Eye View' (烏瞰圖, Ogamdo) implies a perspective from above, looking down, as a crow would. In Limbus Company, it is Yi Sang's base E.G.O and the central metaphor for seeing the world from an alien, elevated vantage that reveals hidden patterns.

literarySources.title: Mirror (거울)
literarySources.author: Yi Sang
literarySources.passage: 거울 속의 나는 왼손잡이이다 — The me in the mirror is left-handed.
literarySources.passageContext: Yi Sang's poem about the double self who cannot be touched, who is opposite yet similar. Directly quoted in Limbus Company promo art and the inspiration for Sang Yi.
literarySources.historicalContext: A short poem about the 'I in the mirror' — a left-handed double who exists in reflection but cannot be grasped. This single image is the genesis of the Mirror technology in Limbus Company: the device named Yeonsim that allows viewing of parallel worlds, and the doppelgänger Sang Yi who embodies the reflected self.

literarySources.title: The Camellias (동백꽃)
literarySources.author: Gim Yujeong
literarySources.passage: Jumsoon pushed me into the camellias and we fell together.
literarySources.passageContext: The moment of falling into blossoms — mirrored in Canto IV when Yi Sang is stabbed by Dongbaek under the Golden Bough's tree branch and literally falls.
literarySources.historicalContext: Gim Yujeong's short story about a village romance conveyed through the camellia flower. The camellia (동백, dongbaek) is the namesake of the League of Nine member who stabs Yi Sang with the Golden Bough, triggering his ego fathoms. Yi Sang's attachment to a rotten burnt potato in Intervallo I also foreshadows Dongbaek — in The Camellias, Jumsoon offers potatoes to get the protagonist's attention.

literarySources.title: The Cow (소)
literarySources.author: Yoo Chi-jin
literarySources.passage: The yellow calf follows its mother through the rain — a scene of innocence before the landlord's reckoning.
literarySources.passageContext: The central symbol of Dongrang's E.G.O Farmwatch: the yellow calf, an emblem of pastoral idealism twisted by the machinery of exploitation.
literarySources.historicalContext: Yoo Chi-jin's play about a farmer's bond with his cow, sold to a landlord who exploits both. The playwright Yoo Chi-jin — pen name Dongrang — was a Guinhoe founding member who initially wrote about farmers' suffering but later became pro-Japanese. In Limbus Company, this betrayal arc is transposed onto Dongrang, who sells out the League under T Corp interrogation and rises to K Corp Branch Manager.

literarySources.title: Glass Window (유리창)
literarySources.author: Jeong Ji-yong
literarySources.passage: 유리창 — through the glass, the dead return as an illusion of warmth.
literarySources.passageContext: Two poems of the same title written in memory of Jeong Ji-yong's dead children. The glass window gives the illusion of resurrecting the dead — the foundation of Young-ji's invention.
literarySources.historicalContext: Jeong Ji-yong wrote two poems titled 'Glass Window' (유리창), both mourning the loss of his children and finding solace only through the pane — a barrier that simultaneously connects and separates. In Limbus Company, Young-ji's Glass Window invention allows the observation of other worlds, offering the same illusory promise of reunion with what is lost. Jeong Ji-yong was later abducted to North Korea; his fate remained unknown for decades — paralleling Young-ji's disappearance.
